title: BEiT: BERT Pre-Training of Image Transformers

## BEIT:

## BERT PRE-TRAINING OF IMAGE TRANSFORMERS

**Hangbo Bao** _[†∗]_ **, Li Dong** _[‡]_ **, Songhao Piao** _[†]_ **, Furu Wei** _[‡]_

_†_ Harbin Institute of Technology

_‡_ Microsoft Research

https://github.com/microsoft/unilm

## ABSTRACT

We introduce a self-supervised vision representation model **BEIT** , which stands for **B** idirectional **E** ncoder representation from **I** mage **T** ransformers. Following BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) developed in the natural language processing area, we propose a _masked image modeling_ task to pretrain vision Transformers. Specifically, each image has two views in our pre-training, i.e., image patches (such as 16 _×_ 16 pixels), and visual tokens (i.e., discrete tokens). We first “tokenize” the original image into visual tokens. Then we randomly mask some image patches and fed them into the backbone Transformer. The pre-training objective is to recover the original visual tokens based on the corrupted image patches. After pre-training BEIT, we directly fine-tune the model parameters on downstream tasks by appending task layers upon the pretrained encoder. Experimental results on image classification and semantic segmentation show that our model achieves competitive results with previous pre-training methods.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) has achieved promising performance in computer vision (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020; Touvron et al., 2020). However, empirical studies show that vision Transformers require more training data than convolutional neural networks. In order to solve the data-hungry issue (Liu et al., 2021a), self-supervised pre-training is a promising solution to leverage large-scale image data. Several strands of methods have been explored for vision Transformers, such as contrastive learning (Chen et al., 2021; Xie et al., 2021), and self-distillation (Caron et al., 2021).

Concurrently, BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) has achieved great success in natural language processing. Its masked language modeling task first randomly masks some proportion of tokens within a text, and then recovers the masked tokens based on the Transformer encoding results of the corrupted text. Motivated by BERT, we turn to the denoising auto-encoding idea to pretrain vision Transformers, which has not been well studied by the vision community. It is challenging to directly apply BERTstyle pre-training for image data. First of all, there is no pre-exist vocabulary for vision Transformer’s input unit, i.e., image patches. So we cannot simply employ a softmax classifier to predict over all possible candidates for masked patches. In contrast, the language vocabulary, such as words and BPE (Sennrich et al., 2016), is well-defined and eases auto-encoding prediction. A straightforward alternative is regarding the task as a regression problem, which predicts the raw pixels of masked patches. However, such pixel-level recovery task tends to waste modeling capability on pre-training short-range dependencies and high-frequency details (Ramesh et al., 2021). Our goal is to overcome the above issues for pre-training of vision Transformers.

In this work, we introduce a self-supervised vision representation model **BEIT** , which stands for **B** idirectional **E** ncoder representation from **I** mage **T** ransformers. Inspired by BERT, we propose a pre-training task, namely, masked image modeling (MIM). As shown in Figure 1, MIM uses two views for each images, i.e., image patches, and visual tokens. We split the image into a grid of patches that are the input representation of backbone Transformer. Moreover, we “tokenize” the image to discrete visual tokens, which is obtained by the latent codes of discrete VAE (Ramesh et al., 2021).

Figure 1: Overview of BEIT pre-training. Before pre-training, we learn an “image tokenizer” via autoencoding-style reconstruction, where an image is tokenized into discrete visual tokens according to the learned vocabulary. During pre-training, each image has two views, i.e., image patches, and visual tokens. We randomly mask some proportion of image patches (gray patches in the figure) and replace them with a special mask embedding [M]. Then the patches are fed to a backbone vision Transformer. The pre-training task aims at predicting the visual tokens of the _original_ image based on the encoding vectors of the _corrupted_ image.

During pre-training, we randomly mask some proportion of image patches, and feed the corrupted input to Transformer. The model learns to recover the visual tokens of the original image, instead of the raw pixels of masked patches.

We perform self-supervised learning and then fine-tune the pretrained BEIT on two downstream tasks, i.e., image classification, and semantic segmentation. Experimental results indicate that BEIT outperforms both from-scratch training and previous strong self-supervised models. Moreover, BEIT is complementary to supervised pre-training. Performance of BEIT can be further improved by intermediate fine-tuning with ImageNet labels. Ablation studies show that our proposed techniques are critical to the effectiveness of BERT-style pre-training for image data. Apart from performance, the improvements of convergence speed and stability of fine-tuning reduce training costs on end tasks. In addition, we demonstrate that self-supervised BEIT can learn reasonable semantic regions via pre-training, unleashing the rich supervision signals contained in images.

Our contributions are summarized as follows:

- We propose a masked image modeling task to pretrain vision Transformers in a self-supervised manner. We also provide a theoretical explanation from the perspective of variational autoencoder.
- We pretrain BEIT and conduct extensive fine-tuning experiments on downstream tasks, such as image classification, and semantic segmentation.
- We present that the self-attention mechanism of self-supervised BEIT learns to distinguish semantic regions and object boundaries, although without using any human annotation.

## 2 METHODS

Given an input image _x_ , BEIT encodes it to contextualized vector representations. As shown in Figure 1, BEIT is pretrained by the masked image modeling (MIM) task in a self-supervised learning manner. MIM aims at recovering the masked image patches based on encoding vectors. For

downstream tasks (such as image classification, and semantic segmentation), we append task layers upon pretrained BEIT and fine-tune the parameters on the specific datasets.

## 2.1 IMAGE REPRESENTATIONS

The images have two views of representations in our method, namely, _image patch_ , and _visual tokens_ . The two types serve as input and output representations during pre-training, respectively.

## 2.1.1 IMAGE PATCH

The 2D image is split into a sequence of patches (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020), so that a standard Transformer can directly accept image data. Formally, we reshape the image _**x** ∈_ R _[H][×][W][ ×][C]_ into _N_ = _HW/P_[2] patches _**x**[p] ∈_ R _[N][×]_[(] _[P]_[ 2] _[C]_[)] , where _C_ is the number of channels, ( _H, W_ ) is the input image resolution, and ( _P, P_ ) is the resolution of each patch. The image patches _{_ _**x**[p] i[}] i[N]_ =1[are flattened] into vectors and are linearly projected, which is similar to word embeddings in BERT (Devlin et al., 2019). Image patches preserve raw pixels and are used as input features in BEIT.
In our experiments, we split each 224 _×_ 224 image into a 14 _×_ 14 grid of image patches, where each patch is 16 _×_ 16.

## 2.1.2 VISUAL TOKEN

Similar to natural language, we represent the image as a sequence of discrete tokens obtained by an “image tokenizer”, instead of raw pixels. Specifically, we tokenize the image _**x** ∈_ R _[H][×][W][ ×][C]_ into _**z**_ = [ _z_ 1 _, . . . , zN_ ] _∈V[h][×][w]_ , where the vocabulary _V_ = _{_ 1 _, . . . , |V|}_ contains discrete token indices.

Following (Ramesh et al., 2021), we use the image tokenizer learned by discrete variational autoencoder (dVAE). There are two modules during visual token learning, namely, _tokenizer_ and _decoder_ . The tokenizer _qφ_ ( _**z** |_ _**x**_ ) maps image pixels _**x**_ into discrete tokens _**z**_ according to a visual codebook (i.e., vocabulary). The decoder _pψ_ ( _**x** |_ _**z**_ ) learns to reconstruct the input image _**x**_ based on the visual tokens _**z**_ . The reconstruction objective can be written as E _**z** ∼qφ_ ( **z** _|_ _**x**_ )[log _pψ_ ( _**x** |_ _**z**_ )]. Because the latent visual tokens are discrete, the model training is non-differentiable. Gumbel-softmax relaxation (Jang et al., 2017; Maddison et al., 2017) is employed to train the model parameters. Moreover, a uniform prior is put on _qφ_ during dVAE training. Refer to (Ramesh et al., 2021) for more training details of the image tokenizer.

We tokenize each image to a 14 _×_ 14 grid of visual tokens. Notice the number of visual tokens and the number of image patches for one image are the same. The vocabulary size is set to _|V|_ = 8192. In our work, we directly use the publicly available[1] image tokenizer described in (Ramesh et al., 2021). We also compare it with a re-implemented tokenizer in Appendix C.

Following ViT (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020), we use the standard Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) as the backbone network. So the results can be directly compared with previous work in terms of the network architecture.

## 2.3 PRE-TRAINING BEIT: MASKED IMAGE MODELING
We propose a _masked image modeling_ (MIM) task. We randomly mask some percentage of image patches, and then predict the visual tokens that are corresponding to the masked patches.

Rather than randomly choosing patches for the masked positions _M_ , we employ blockwise masking in our work. As summarized in Algorithm 1, a block of image patches is masked each time. The MIM task is greatly inspired by masked language modeling (Devlin et al., 2019), which is one of the most successful pre-training objective in natural language processing. Moreover, blockwise (or n-gram) masking is also widely applied in BERT-like models (Joshi et al., 2020; Bao et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020). However, directly using pixel-level auto-encoding (i.e., recovering the pixels of masked patches) for vision pre-training pushes the model to focus on short-range dependencies and high-frequency details (Ramesh et al., 2021). BEIT overcomes the above issue by predicting discrete visual tokens, which summarizes the details to high-level abstractions. Ablation studies in Section 3.3 show that our proposed method significantly outperforms pixel-level auto-encoding.

## 2.5 PRE-TRAINING SETUP

The network architecture of BEIT follows that of ViT-Base (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020) for a fair comparison. We use a 12-layer Transformer with 768 hidden size, and 12 attention heads. The intermediate size of feed-forward networks is 3072. We employ the default 16 _×_ 16 input patch size. We directly borrow the image tokenizer trained by Ramesh et al. (2021). The vocabulary size of visual tokens is 8192.

We pretrain BEIT on the training set of ImageNet-1K (Russakovsky et al., 2015), which contains about 1 _._ 2M images. Our augmentation policy includes random resized cropping, horizontal flipping, color jittering (Wu et al., 2018). Notice that we do not use the labels for self-supervised learning. We use the 224 _×_ 224 resolution in our experiments. So the input is split to 14 _×_ 14 image patches, and the same amount of visual tokens. We randomly mask at most 75 patches (i.e., roughly 40% of total image patches).

The pre-training runs for about 500k steps (i.e., 800 epochs) with 2k batch size. Adam (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2019) with _β_ 1 = 0 _._ 9 _, β_ 2 = 0 _._ 999 is employed for optimization. The learning rate is set to 1.5e-3, with a warmup of 10 epochs, and cosine learning rate decay. The weight decay is 0 _._ 05. We employ stochastic depth (Huang et al., 2016) with a 0 _._ 1 rate, and disable dropout. The 500k training steps take about five days using 16 Nvidia Telsa V100 32GB GPU cards.

We find that proper initialization is important to stabilize Transformer, especially for large-scale pretraining. We first randomly initialize all the parameters within a small range, such as [ _−_ 0 _._ 02 _,_ 0 _._ 02]. Then, for the _l_ -th Transformer layer, we rescale the output matrices (i.e., the last linear projection within each sub-layer) of the self-attention module and the feed-forward network by ~~_√_~~ 12 _l_[.]

## 2.6 FINE-TUNING BEIT ON DOWNSTREAM VISION TASKS

After pre-training BEIT, we append a task layer upon the Transformer, and fine-tune the parameters on downstream tasks, like BERT. We take image classification and semantic segmentation as examples in our work. It is straightforward to leverage the pre-training-then-fine-tuning paradigm on other vision tasks with BEIT.

**Image classification.** For image classification tasks, we directly employ a simple linear classifier as the task layer. Specifically, we use average pooling to aggregate the representations, and feed the global to a softmax classifier. The category probabilities are computed as softmax(avg( _{_ _**h**[L] i[}][N] i_ =1 _**[W]**[c]_[))][,][where] _**[h]**[L] i_[is][the][final][encoding][vector][of][the] _[i]_[-th][image][patch,] _**W** c ∈_ R _[D][×][C]_ is a parameter matrix, and _C_ is the number of labels. We maximize the likelihood of labeled data by updating the parameters of BEIT and the softmax classifier.

**Semantic segmentation.** For semantic segmentation, we follow the task layer used in SETRPUP (Zheng et al., 2020). To be specific, we use pretrained BEIT as a backbone encoder, and incorporate several deconvolution layers as decoder to produce segmentation. The model is also end-to-end fine-tuned similar to image classification.

**Intermediate fine-tuning.** After self-supervised pre-training, we can further train BEIT on a datarich intermediate dataset (i.e., ImageNet-1K in our work), and then finetune the model on the target downstream tasks. Such intermediate fine-tuning is the common practice of BERT fine-tuning in NLP (Pruksachatkun et al., 2020). We directly follow the method for BEIT.

## 3 EXPERIMENTS

We conduct full fine-tuning experiments on image classification and semantic segmentation. Moreover, we present various ablation studies for pre-training and analyze the representations learned by BEIT. We also report linear probes on ImageNet in Appendix D.

## 3.1 IMAGE CLASSIFICATION

The image classification task classifies input images to various categories. We evaluate BEIT on the ILSVRC-2012 ImageNet dataset (Russakovsky et al., 2015) with 1k classes and 1 _._ 3M images. We directly follow the most of hyperparameters of DeiT (Touvron et al., 2020) in our fine-tuning experiments for a fair comparison. We reduce fine-tuning epochs compared with training from scratch, as BEIT has been pre-trained. Accordingly, we use a larger learning rate with layer-wise decay. The detailed hyperparameters are summarized in Appendix H.

Table 1 reports top-1 accuracy on image classification. We compare BEIT with vision Transformers trained by random initialization, supervised pre-training, and previous self-supervised learning methods. All the compared models are base-size, except iGPT has 1.36B parameters. Pre-training is conducted on ImageNet for the comparison purpose, except ViT-JFT300M is pretrained on Google’s in-house 300M images.

Compared with the models trained by random initialization, we find that pre-trained BEIT significantly improves performance on both datasets. BEIT improves the performance on ImageNet, which shows the effectiveness under the rich-resource setting.

Moreover, we compare BEIT with previous state-of-the-art self-supervised methods for Transformer, such as DINO (Caron et al., 2021), and MoCo v3 (Chen et al., 2021). Our proposed method outperforms previous models on ImageNet fine-tuning. Among them, iGPT-1.36B (Chen et al., 2020a) uses much more parameters (i.e., 1 _._ 36B vs 86M), and ViT-JFT300M (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020) is pretrained on larger corpus (i.e., 300M vs 1 _._ 3M), while others pretrain ViT-Base on ImageNet-1K. iGPT-1.36B and ViT-JFT300M are the most comparable methods, which also follows auto-encoding pre-training for vision Transformer. Specifically, iGPT uses clustered image tokens as both input and output for image GPT or image BERT. In contrast, we use image patches as input to preserve raw pixels, and employ discrete visual tokens as a prediction bottleneck. ViT-JFT300 predicts the mean, 3-bit color of each masked patch, rather than visual tokens learned by discrete VAE. We also pretrain the self-supervised tasks of BEIT and DINO in a multi-task learning manner, which is presented in Appendix E.

In addition, we evaluate our proposed method with intermediate fine-tuning. In other words, we first pretrain BEIT in a self-supervised manner, and then fine-tune the pretrained model on ImageNet with labeled data. The results show that BEIT is complementary to supervised pre-training, achieving additional gain after intermediate fine-tuning on ImageNet.

**Fine-tuning to** 384 _×_ 384 **resolution.** After fine-tuning with resolution 224 _×_ 224, we additionally fine-tune the model on 384 _×_ 384 images by 10 more epochs. We follow the standard higher-resolution setting of DeiT (Touvron et al., 2020), except using fewer epochs. Notice that we keep patch size the same for both 224 _×_ 224 and 384 _×_ 384 images. So the input sequence length of Transformers becomes longer for higher resolutions. Table 1 shows that higher resolution improves the BEIT results by 1+ points on ImageNet. More importantly, BEIT384 pretrained on ImageNet-1K even outperforms supervised pre-training ViT384 that uses ImageNet-22K, when they use the same input resolution.

**Scaling up to larger size.** We further scale up BEIT to the large size (same as ViT-L). As shown in Table 1, ViT384-L is worse than ViT384 on ImageNet, when training from scratch. The results verifies the data-hungry issue of vision Transformers. Supervised pre-training on ImageNet-22K partially relieves the issue, where ViT384-L finally outperforms ViT384 by 1 _._ 2. In comparison, BEIT-L is better than BEIT by 2 _._ 0, and BEIT384-L outperforms BEIT384 by 1 _._ 7. In other words, the benefits of scaling up BEIT from base to large are greater than supervised pre-training with ImageNet-22K. More importantly, comparing between BEIT384 with ViT384 that conducts supervised pre-training on ImageNet-22K, the improvements of BEIT become greater along with scaling the size from base

## 3.2 SEMANTIC SEGMENTATION

Semantic segmentation aims to predict a corresponding class for each pixel of the input image. We evaluate BEIT on the ADE20K benchmark (Zhou et al., 2019) with 25K images and 150 semantic categories. We report the metric of mean Intersection of Union (mIoU) averaged over all semantic categories. As presented in Section 2.6, we directly follow the task layer and the most of hyperparameters described in SETR-PUP (Zheng et al., 2020). On ADE20K, we use Adam (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2019) as the optimizer. The learning rate is set to 1e-3 with layer-wise decay similar to image classification. We conduct fine-tuning for 160K steps. The batch size is 16. The detailed hyperparameters are described in Appendix I.

As shown in Table 3, we compare BEIT with supervised pre-training that relies on labeled data of ImageNet. We find that our proposed method achieves better performance than supervised pretraining, although BEIT does not require manual annotations for pre-training. Moreover, we employ intermediate fine-tuning for BEIT on ImageNet, i.e., we first fine-tune pretrained BEIT on ImageNet, and then fine-tune the model on ADE20K. The results indicate that intermediate fine-tuning further improves BEIT on semantic segmentation.

## 3.3 ABLATION STUDIES

We conduct ablation studies to analyze the contributions of each component in BEIT. The models are evaluated on image classification (i.e., ImageNet) and semantic segmentation (i.e., ADE20K). We set the default pre-training steps to 300 epochs for the ablation studies, which is 37 _._ 5% of the total steps used in the previous experiments.

Table 4 reports the results of various model variants. First, we ablate blockwise masking by randomly sample masked positions. We find that blockwise masking is beneficial on both tasks, especially on semantic segmentation. Second, we ablate the usage of visual tokens by predicting the raw pixels of masked patches, i.e., the pre-training task becomes a pixel regression problem to recover masked patches. Our proposed masked image modeling task significantly outperforms naive pixel-level auto-encoding. Compared with the results in Table 1, the ablation result is worse than training vision Transformer from scratch on two tasks. The results indicate that the prediction of visual tokens is the key ingredient of BEIT. Third, we ablate the usage of visual tokens and blockwise masking together. We find that blockwise masking is even more helpful for pixel-level auto-encoding, which relieves the suffering of short-distance dependency. Forth, recovering all the visual tokens harms performance on downstream tasks. Fifth, we compare BEIT with different training steps. Pre-training the model longer can further improve performance on downstream tasks.

## 3.4 ANALYSIS OF SELF-ATTENTION MAP

We show that the self-attention mechanism in BEIT can separate objects, even though our pre-training does not rely on any manual annotation at all. Similar properties are also observed by Caron et al. (2021). The probing images are taken from the MS COCO (Lin et al., 2014) corpus to avoid appearing in the pre-training data.

Figure 2: Self-attention map for different reference points. The self-attention mechanism in BEIT is able to separate objects, although self-supervised pre-training does not use manual annotations.

As shown in Figure 2, we plot the self-attention map for different reference points within an image. The visualizations are produced by attention scores computed via query-key product in the last layer. For each reference point, we use the corresponding patch as query, and show which patch it attends to. After pre-training, BEIT learns to distinguish semantic regions using self-attention heads, without any task-specific supervision. The property partially indicates the reason why BEIT is able to help downstream tasks. Such knowledge acquired by BEIT potentially improves the generalization ability of fine-tuned models, especially on small-scale datasets.

## 4 RELATED WORK

**Self-supervised visual representation learning.** Various methods have been introduced over the years to pretrain vision models in a self-supervised manner. Pioneering works design clever pretext tasks, such as predicting the patch orderings (Noroozi & Favaro, 2016), colorization (Zhang et al., 2016), and predicting rotation angles (Komodakis & Gidaris, 2018). In addition, Trinh et al. (2019) propose to mask some patches within an image, and classify whether the masked patches are real or fake for each masked position. The method is similar to the masked version of Jigsaw pretraining (Noroozi & Favaro, 2016). The recent strand of research follows contrastive paradigm (Wu et al., 2018; Oord et al., 2018; Hjelm et al., 2019; Bachman et al., 2019; He et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020b;c). The models typically regard various data augmentations as different views of an image, and then make the representations of positive pairs similar while pushing negative pairs away. In order to obtain enough informative negative samples in contrastive learning, the methods usually rely on large memory banks (Wu et al., 2018; He et al., 2020) or large batch size (Chen et al., 2020b). BYOL (Grill et al., 2020) and SimSiam (Chen & He, 2020) further eliminate the requirement of negative samples, using various techniques to avoid representation collapse. Another strand of methods use clustering to organize image examples (Caron et al., 2018; Asano et al., 2020; Caron et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021).

**Self-supervised vision Transformers.** Pre-training vision Transformers has received significant attention recently due to the data-hungry issue. iGPT (Chen et al., 2020a) first creates a 9-bit color palette by k-means clustering RGB pixels, and then uses the clustered tokens to represent images. Next iGPT uses the tasks of BERT and GPT to pretrain Transformers. In comparison, our proposed method uses image patches as input without losing pixel-level information. Moreover, our visual tokens are obtained by discrete VAE instead of clustering. ViT (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020) conducts a preliminary exploration with the masked patch prediction task, which predicts the 3-bit mean color of the masked patches. Dosovitskiy et al. (2020) also report that pixel-level auto-encoding performs

9

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

worse, although it is the most straightforward translation of BERT from NLP to CV. Rather than using heuristically designed pre-training tasks, our proposed model leverages visual tokens learned by discrete VAE, which not only achieves better performance but also is better theoretically motivated. Apart from masked auto-encoding, other mainstream research works use contrastive learning (Chen et al., 2021; Xie et al., 2021), and self-distillation (Caron et al., 2021). In comparison, BEIT can achieve several times of improvement in terms of pre-training throughput (Appendix E), and memory consumption. The advantages make BEIT appealing to scale up vision Transformers.

## 5 CONCLUSION

We introduce a self-supervised pre-training framework for vision Transformers, achieving strong fine-tuning results on downstream tasks, such as image classification, and semantic segmentation. We show that the proposed method is critical to make BERT-like pre-training (i.e., auto-encoding with masked input) work well for image Transformers. We also present the intriguing property of automatically acquired knowledge about semantic regions, without using any human-annotated data. In the future, we would like to scale up BEIT pre-training in terms of data size and model size. Moreover, we will conduct multimodal pre-training in a more unified way, using the similar objectives and the shared architecture for texts and images.

coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3-flash-preview)
coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper presents BEiT, a self-supervised pre-training method for Vision Transformers that adapts the BERT masked language modeling paradigm to the visual domain using discrete visual tokens. By predicting the indices of masked patches from a pre-trained dVAE codebook rather than raw pixels, the model learns high-level semantic representations that transfer effectively to classification and segmentation tasks.

The paper makes a significant contribution by demonstrating that discrete token prediction is a viable and potentially superior alternative to pixel-level reconstruction for masked image modeling. It achieves impressive results on ImageNet-1K and ADE20K, showing that the BERT-style objective can scale Vision Transformers effectively without relying on contrastive pairs or large batch sizes.

Overview issues:

- Dependency on Pre-trained Tokenizer Quality: The methodology in Section 2.1.2 relies entirely on a frozen dVAE tokenizer trained by a third party (Ramesh et al., 2021). This creates a potential bottleneck where the Transformer's representation capacity is capped by the semantic richness of the dVAE's 8192-entry codebook. If the tokenizer fails to capture certain textures or structures during its own training on a different dataset, the BEiT encoder may never learn to represent those features regardless of the pre-training scale. The paper lacks an analysis of how the choice of tokenizer or its vocabulary size affects the final downstream performance, leaving it unclear if the gains come from the MIM task itself or the specific properties of the DALL-E tokenizer.

- Theoretical Gap in Discrete vs. Continuous Reconstruction: Section 2.4 attempts to frame BEiT as a Variational Autoencoder, but the justification for why discrete tokens are superior to pixel regression is largely empirical rather than theoretical. The authors claim in Section 2.3 that pixel-level recovery 'wastes modeling capability on high-frequency details,' yet they do not provide evidence that the dVAE tokenizer successfully filters only 'useful' information while discarding noise. Without a more rigorous comparison or a visualization of what information is lost during tokenization, the claim that visual tokens are the 'correct' abstraction for BERT-style vision pre-training remains an unproven assumption.

- Block-wise Masking Necessity in Vision: The authors introduce block-wise masking in Section 2.3 and Algorithm 1, claiming it prevents the model from exploiting local correlations. While Table 4 shows a drop in performance when using random sampling, the paper does not explore the interaction between patch size (16x16) and masking strategy. Since images have high spatial redundancy, a 40% random mask might still leave enough adjacent pixel information for the model to 'cheat' via simple interpolation. The paper needs to clarify whether block-wise masking is a fundamental requirement for visual BERT models or just a hyperparameter optimization for the specific 16x16 patch resolution used.

- Limited Evaluation of Feature Granularity: The results in Table 3 for semantic segmentation are promising, but the paper does not investigate if the discrete bottleneck limits performance on dense prediction tasks requiring sub-patch precision. Since the MIM task predicts one token per 16x16 block, there is a risk that the model becomes invariant to intra-patch details that are necessary for precise boundary detection in segmentation. While Section 3.4 shows attention maps separating objects, a quantitative analysis of boundary adherence or performance on small-object detection would be necessary to prove that the discrete token approach doesn't sacrifice spatial resolution for semantic abstraction.

- Incomplete Discussion of Pre-training Efficiency: Section 2.5 notes that pre-training takes five days on 16 V100 GPUs for 800 epochs. However, the paper compares BEiT to contrastive methods like MoCo v3 and DINO without a standardized 'accuracy-vs-compute' metric. Because BEiT requires a two-stage process (training/running a dVAE followed by the Transformer), the total wall-clock time and computational footprint are higher than end-to-end methods. The claim in Section 4 that BEiT is 'appealing to scale up' needs to be supported by a more detailed breakdown of the total training cost, including the tokenizer overhead, compared to contemporary self-supervised frameworks.

- Missing comparison to the original ViT masked patch prediction result: The paper positions BEiT as the first successful adaptation of BERT to vision, yet Dosovitskiy et al. (2020) previously reported results for a 'masked patch prediction' task. While Section 4 mentions this, the paper never formally demonstrates that BEiT recovers or exceeds that specific baseline under identical conditions. To show the result has bite, the authors should include a direct comparison in Table 1 or a dedicated experiment using the 3-bit mean color prediction target on the same ViT-Base architecture. Without this, it is difficult to determine if the performance gain stems from the dVAE tokens or simply from the modern training recipe used in this work.

- Absence of a worked example for tokenizer generalization: The authors use a tokenizer trained by a third party on a different data distribution, but they do not demonstrate that this discrete vocabulary is non-vacuous for the ImageNet-1K pre-training set. A significant gap exists in showing whether the 8192 tokens actually represent diverse semantic concepts across the ImageNet classes. The paper should include a visualization where clusters of image patches from the ImageNet validation set are grouped by their assigned visual token index. Showing that a specific token consistently corresponds to a semantic feature, like 'bird beaks' or 'car wheels,' would validate the assumption that these tokens serve as meaningful 'visual words' for the Transformer.

- Underdeveloped implementation guidance for different patch resolutions: The paper claims the MIM task is a general framework, yet all results and the masking strategy in Algorithm 1 are tuned for 16x16 patches. Readers will wonder if the 40% masking ratio and the block size $s$ in Algorithm 1 must scale linearly with patch resolution to maintain the same difficulty level. The paper lacks a discussion or a small-scale experiment showing how to adapt the block-wise masking parameters for 8x8 or 32x32 patches. Providing a sensitivity analysis of the masking ratio relative to patch size is necessary for the method to be practically implementable for researchers using different Transformer configurations.

- Missing verification of the VAE theoretical framework: Section 2.4 provides a theoretical derivation from the ELBO, but the paper does not verify if the MIM objective actually optimizes the bound in a way that correlates with reconstruction quality. A natural check is to compute the 'Visual Token Reconstruction' term and the 'Masked Image Modeling' term from Equation 3 during training. The paper should show a plot of these two components to demonstrate that as the MIM loss decreases, the model is indeed learning a tighter bound on the image likelihood. This would bridge the gap between the abstract mathematical motivation and the empirical cross-entropy loss used in the experiments.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Include an ablation study or analysis on the impact of different visual tokenizers (e.g., varying vocabulary sizes or using a tokenizer trained on a different domain).

- Provide a quantitative comparison of intra-patch reconstruction accuracy to justify the claim that pixel-level modeling focuses too much on 'high-frequency details' compared to tokens.

- Add a more detailed computational complexity analysis that accounts for the two-stage nature of the pipeline (dVAE + Transformer) relative to single-stage contrastive methods.

- Expand the semantic segmentation analysis to include metrics on boundary precision to ensure the 16x16 token bottleneck does not degrade fine-grained spatial information.

comments.title: Dimension mismatch in position embedding addition
comments.quote: The input vectors $\bm{H}_{0}=[\bm{e}_{\texttt{[S]}},\bm{E}\bm{x}^{p}_{i},\ldots,\bm{E}\bm{x}^{p}_{N}]+\bm{E}_{pos}$ is fed into Transformer.
comments.explanation: There is a dimensionality mismatch in the addition of position embeddings. The sequence length of the input vectors is $N+1$ because it includes the special token [S] and $N$ image patches, but the position embedding matrix $\bm{E}_{pos}$ is defined in the previous sentence as $\mathbb{R}^{N\times D}$. Adding a matrix of size $N \times D$ to a sequence of length $N+1$ is undefined. The position embedding matrix should be defined as $\bm{E}_{pos}\in\mathbb{R}^{(N+1)\times D}$ to account for the [S] token.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Pixel Regression Baseline Performance
comments.quote: Second, we ablate the usage of visual tokens by predicting the raw pixels of masked patches, i.e., the pre-training task becomes a pixel regression problem to recover masked patches. Our proposed masked image modeling task significantly outperforms naive pixel-level auto-encoding. Compared with the results in Table 1, the ablation result is worse than training vision Transformer from scratch on two tasks.
comments.explanation: The text claims that the pixel regression ablation (81.04% Top-1 in Table 4) is worse than training from scratch. However, Table 1 lists the 'DeiT-B (from scratch)' baseline as 81.8%. This means the pixel-level pre-training actually provides a gain over the scratch baseline, contradicting the statement that it is 'worse than training vision Transformer from scratch'. This error matters because it overstates the necessity of visual tokens by suggesting that pixel regression is actively harmful compared to random initialization, when the data shows it is merely less effective than tokens.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Distribution Simplification for Equation 3
comments.quote:
e keeping $q_{\phi}$ and $p_{\psi}$ fixed. We simplify $q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}|x_{i})$ to a one-point distribution with the most likely visual tokens $\hat{z}_{i}=\arg\max_{z}q_{\phi}(z|x_{i})$. Then Equation (2) can be rewritten as:

$\sum_{(x_{i},\tilde{x}_{i})\in\mathcal{D}}\big{(}\underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{z_{i}\sim q_{\phi}(z|x_{i})}[\log p_{\psi}(x_{i}|z_{i})]}_{\text{Stage 1: Visual Token Reconstruction}}\quad+\underbrace{\log p_{\theta}(\hat{z}_{i}|\tilde{x}_{i})}_{\text{Stage 2: Masked Image Modeling}}\big{)}$
comments.explanation: The derivation of Equation (3) from Equation (2) contains a logical inconsistency. If $q_{\phi}(\mathbf{z}|x_{i})$ is simplified to a one-point distribution $\delta(z - \hat{z}_{i})$, then the expectation $\mathbb{E}_{z_{i}\sim q_{\phi}}[\log p_{\psi}(x_{i}|z_{i})]$ mathematically collapses to $\log p_{\psi}(x_{i}|\hat{z}_{i})$. By retaining the expectation in the first term of (3) while using the point estimate $\hat{z}_i$ in the second term, the notation suggests $q_{\phi}$ is simultaneously a full distribution and a delta distribution. The first term of Equation (3) should be rewritten as $\log p_{\psi}(x_{i}|\hat{z}_{i})$ to maintain consistency with the one-point distribution assumption.

comments.title: Logical Gap in Aspect Ratio and Block Size Constraints
comments.quote:
$a\leftarrow\sqrt{s\cdot r};b\leftarrow\sqrt{s/r}$
$t\leftarrow\text{Rand}(0,h-a);l\leftarrow\text{Rand}(0,w-b)$
comments.explanation: The algorithm does not account for cases where the sampled dimensions $a$ or $b$ exceed the image boundaries $h$ or $w$. While $s$ is capped at $0.4N$, a very high or low aspect ratio $r$ can result in $a > h$ or $b > w$. For example, if $s=100$ and $r=3.33$, then $a \approx 18.2$; if the image height $h=14$, the sampling range for $t$ becomes negative. The algorithm needs explicit clamping or rejection sampling to ensure $a \le h$ and $b \le w$ before sampling the top-left corner $(t, l)$.

comments.title: Incomplete ELBO formulation for dVAE training
comments.quote: The reconstruction objective can be written as $\mathbb{E}_{\bm{z}\sim q_{\phi}(\bm{z}|\bm{x})}[\log p_{\psi}(\bm{x}|\bm{z})]$. Because the latent visual tokens are discrete, the model training is non-differentiable. Gumbel-softmax relaxation *(Jang et al., 2017; Maddison et al., 2017)* is employed to train the model parameters. Moreover, a uniform prior is put on $q_{\phi}$ during dVAE training.
comments.explanation: The text defines the objective solely as the reconstruction term, which is mathematically insufficient for a Variational Autoencoder (VAE). The Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) for a dVAE is defined as $\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(\bm{z}|\bm{x})}[\log p_{\psi}(\bm{x}|\bm{z})] - D_{KL}(q_{\phi}(\bm{z}|\bm{x}) || p(\bm{z}))$. Omitting the KL divergence term from the objective statement implies a standard autoencoder with a bottleneck, rather than the probabilistic framework required for the Gumbel-softmax relaxation to be theoretically grounded. In the discrete case with a uniform prior, the KL term is essential for preventing the posterior from collapsing into a deterministic mapping.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Layer-wise Scaling Initialization
comments.quote: Then, for the $l$-th Transformer layer, we rescale the output matrices (i.e., the last linear projection within each sub-layer) of the self-attention module and the feed-forward network by $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2l}}$.
comments.explanation: The initialization description is ambiguous regarding the index $l$. In standard Transformer implementations, $l$ can be 0-indexed or 1-indexed. If $l=0$ for the first layer, the scaling factor $1/\sqrt{0}$ is undefined. If $l$ starts at 1, the first layer is scaled by $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$, while the 12th layer is scaled by $1/\sqrt{24} \approx 0.204$. This significantly dampens the residual branches of deeper layers. Without specifying the starting index, a reader cannot replicate the variance stabilization intended by the authors.

comments.title: Transposed dimensions in linear projection matrix
comments.quote: The patches are then linearly projected to obtain patch embeddings $\bm{E}\bm{x}^{p}_{i}$, where $\bm{E}\in\mathbb{R}^{(P^{2}C)\times D}$.
comments.explanation: The dimensions of the projection matrix $\bm{E}$ are reversed relative to the standard matrix-vector multiplication $\bm{E}\bm{x}^{p}_{i}$. If $\bm{x}^{p}_{i}$ is a flattened patch vector of size $P^2C$, and the resulting embedding must be of size $D$ for the Transformer, the matrix $\bm{E}$ must have $D$ rows and $P^2C$ columns. The current definition $\mathbb{R}^{(P^{2}C)\times D}$ would result in an output vector of size $P^2C$. The definition should be $\bm{E}\in\mathbb{R}^{D\times (P^{2}C)}$ to ensure the output embedding has the correct hidden dimension $D$.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Masking Ratio and Patch Count
comments.quote: So the input is split to $14\times 14$ image patches, and the same amount of visual tokens. We randomly mask at most $75$ patches (i.e., roughly $40\%$ of total image patches).
comments.explanation: The math regarding the masking ratio is inconsistent. A $14\times 14$ grid results in 196 total patches. Masking 75 patches constitutes $75 / 196 \approx 38.27\%$. While the text describes this as 'roughly 40%', the paper later emphasizes the importance of the block-wise masking strategy which targets a specific budget. If the budget is capped at 75, the model is seeing more of the image than a standard 40% mask would allow. This discrepancy matters because the difficulty of the pretext task is sensitive to the masking percentage.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a well-motivated and timely contribution by adapting BERT-style masked modeling to vision Transformers. The core idea of using discrete visual tokens as targets for masked image modeling is conceptually strong and aligns with emerging best practices in self-supervised learning. The experimental evaluation is thorough across classification and segmentation tasks, and the ablation studies effectively isolate key components. The method achieves competitive performance while offering a compelling alternative to contrastive approaches.

Overview issues:

- Overstated theoretical connection to VAEs: The paper frames BEiT's pre-training objective as a variational autoencoder (VAE) in Section 2.4, citing the ELBO derivation to justify the approach. However, the actual training procedure does not optimize the full VAE objective—only the prior modeling term $\log p_\theta(\hat{z}_i|\tilde{x}_i)$ is trained during MIM, while the tokenizer $q_\phi$ and decoder $p_\psi$ are frozen. This disconnects the theoretical motivation from practice: the ELBO interpretation requires joint or alternating optimization of both reconstruction and prior terms, which is not done. The VAE framing risks misleading readers about the actual learning dynamics. A more accurate description would be that BEiT uses a pretrained discrete tokenizer and trains a contextual prior over it—without claiming full VAE convergence. The derivation in Equation (3) is algebraically correct under the one-point approximation, but the broader narrative oversells the VAE connection.

- Insufficient analysis of tokenizer limitations: The method relies entirely on a fixed dVAE tokenizer from Ramesh et al. (2021), yet provides no investigation into how its properties affect downstream performance. The tokenizer may introduce quantization errors, semantic ambiguities, or distributional shifts—especially on images dissimilar to ImageNet. While Appendix C mentions a reimplementation, there is no analysis of token distribution, coverage, or failure modes (e.g., rare textures or fine-grained categories). Given that visual tokens serve as the supervision signal, their quality directly impacts model performance. Readers will wonder whether poor tokenization could explain performance ceilings or domain transfer issues. The paper should include qualitative examples of tokenization outputs, token frequency statistics, or robustness tests under distribution shift to support the assumption that these tokens are reliable semantic proxies.

- Blockwise masking benefits lack mechanistic explanation: Ablation results in Table 4 show that blockwise masking improves performance, especially in semantic segmentation. However, the paper does not explain why this is the case beyond citing NLP practices. Is it because larger masked regions force the model to rely on longer-range context? Or does it prevent overfitting to local textures? Without analysis—such as attention pattern visualization under different masking schemes or controlled studies varying block size—the mechanism remains speculative. This is a missed opportunity: understanding how masking structure influences representation learning could guide future designs. The authors should analyze whether blockwise masking leads to more global attention maps or better object-level coherence compared to random patch masking.

- Claimed 'bidirectionality' is structurally misleading: The title and framing emphasize 'bidirectional' encoding, drawing a direct analogy to BERT. But unlike text, where word order enforces a linear sequence with clear left-right context, images lack an intrinsic ordering. The 2D position embeddings in ViT do provide spatial context, but the model does not inherently distinguish forward/backward dependencies as in language. Calling this 'bidirectional' risks overclaiming linguistic parallels that don’t hold structurally. The self-attention mechanism is context-aware, but not directional in the way BERT’s is. A more precise framing would emphasize context aggregation or dense prediction rather than bidirectionality. This distinction matters because it affects how we interpret the model’s inductive biases and its ability to capture compositional structure.

- Missing comparison with concurrent masked reconstruction baselines: The paper positions BEiT against contrastive methods like DINO and MoCo v3, but does not compare with other masked modeling approaches that reconstruct pixels—such as MAE (He et al., 2022), which emerged around the same time. MAE shows that pixel reconstruction with high masking ratios can work well when combined with asymmetric encoder-decoder design. By only comparing to older pixel-level baselines (e.g., ViT’s masked patch prediction), the paper underestimates the competitiveness of alternative reconstruction objectives. A direct comparison with MAE using similar training settings would clarify whether discrete tokens are truly necessary or if architectural choices dominate. Given that MAE achieves strong results with simpler objectives, this omission weakens the argument for the necessity of visual tokenization.

- No analysis of visual token semantic fidelity: The paper assumes that discrete visual tokens from a dVAE capture meaningful semantic content, but provides no evidence that these tokens align with human-interpretable image structures. Readers will want to know whether tokens correspond to edges, textures, object parts, or whole objects, and whether rare or fine-grained categories are poorly represented. This matters because the entire pre-training signal depends on token prediction accuracy. To support the claim that visual tokens enable semantic recovery, the authors should compute token consistency across transformations (e.g., rotation, cropping) and visualize the most frequent/least frequent tokens on ImageNet, linking high-frequency tokens to semantic categories.

- Missing ablation on masking ratio and block size: While blockwise masking is used with a 40% masking ratio, the paper does not explore how performance varies with these choices. A standard expectation for masked modeling papers is to test a range of masking ratios (e.g., 20% to 75%) and block sizes (e.g., 1×1 vs. 4×4 patches) to show robustness and guide future applications. This is especially important because the optimal masking strategy may differ between classification and segmentation. The authors should add a table showing ImageNet accuracy and ADE20K mIoU across at least three masking ratios and two block configurations to justify their default setting.

- No evaluation on low-data fine-tuning regimes: The paper claims BEiT improves generalization, but all fine-tuning experiments use full datasets (e.g., full ImageNet, full ADE20K). It does not test whether the learned representations help when labeled data is scarce—a key use case for self-supervised pre-training. Without this, the claim of improved generalization lacks bite. To demonstrate real-world utility, the authors should evaluate few-shot or low-data fine-tuning (e.g., 1%, 5%, 10% of ImageNet) and compare BEiT against supervised pre-training and other self-supervised methods in these settings.

- Lack of cross-dataset generalization analysis: The model is pretrained and evaluated on ImageNet and ADE20K, which share distributional overlap. There is no test on out-of-domain datasets (e.g., medical images, satellite imagery, or non-natural images like sketches) to assess whether BEiT learns transferable features beyond its pre-training distribution. Given the reliance on a fixed tokenizer trained on natural images, performance may degrade sharply off-distribution. To validate broad applicability, the authors should report results on at least one domain-shifted benchmark such as ImageNet-R or STL-10, including tokenizer reconstruction quality on such data.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Revise the VAE section to accurately reflect that only the prior is trained, not the full ELBO, and downplay claims of full VAE equivalence.

- Add analysis of the visual tokenizer’s behavior, including qualitative examples and token distribution statistics, to justify its use as a semantic proxy.

- Investigate the mechanism behind blockwise masking through attention visualization or ablation by block size to explain its benefits.

- Reframe 'bidirectionality' to avoid misleading linguistic analogies and instead emphasize context aggregation in 2D space.

- Include a comparison with MAE or similar recent masked reconstruction methods to better position BEiT’s contribution relative to concurrent work.

comments.title: Incorrect set notation for masked positions
comments.quote: We randomly mask approximately $40\%$ image patches, where the masked positions are denoted as $\mathcal{M} \subset \{1, \dots, N\}^{0.4N}$.
comments.explanation: The expression $\{1, \dots, N\}^{0.4N}$ is mathematically incorrect—this notation typically denotes a function space, not a subset of indices. The intended meaning is that $\mathcal{M}$ is a subset of $\{1, \dots, N\}$ with size approximately $0.4N$. The current formulation misrepresents the masking process and could confuse readers about the structure of $\mathcal{M}$. Rewrite as: 'We randomly mask approximately 40% of image patches, where the masked positions are denoted as $\mathcal{M} \subset \{1, \dots, N\}$ with $|\mathcal{M}| \approx 0.4N$.'

comments.title: Misleading union in corrupted patch definition
comments.quote: The corrupted image patches $x^{\mathcal{M}}=\{\bm{x}_{i}^{p}:i\notin\mathcal{M}\}_{i=1}^{N}\bigcup\{\bm{e}_{\left|\mathcal{M}\right|}:i\in\mathcal{M}\}_{i=1}^{N}$ are then fed into the $L$-layer Transformer as described in Section 2.2.
comments.explanation: Using $\bigcup$ to combine two indexed sets is invalid—this operator applies to sets, not ordered sequences. The current expression fails to define how masked and unmasked patches are reassembled spatially, and the double indexing over $i=1$ to $N$ creates ambiguity. The model expects an ordered sequence of patches, so the definition must preserve spatial layout. Replace with a conditional definition: 'The corrupted image patches $x^{\mathcal{M}} = (\bm{y}_i)_{i=1}^N$, where $\bm{y}_i = \bm{x}_i^p$ if $i \notin \mathcal{M}$ and $\bm{y}_i = \bm{e}_{|\mathcal{M}|}$ if $i \in \mathcal{M}$, are fed into the Transformer.'

comments.title: Incorrect position embedding dimensionality
comments.quote: We also add standard learnable 1D position embeddings $\bm{E}_{pos}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times D}$ to patch embeddings. The input vectors $\bm{H}_{0}=[\bm{e}_{\texttt{[S]}},\bm{E}\bm{x}^{p}_{1},\ldots,\bm{E}\bm{x}^{p}_{N}]+\bm{E}_{pos}$ is fed into Transformer.
comments.explanation: The input sequence includes the prepended [S] token, so it has length $N+1$, but the position embeddings are defined as $\mathbb{R}^{N \times D}$, creating a dimension mismatch. This would prevent correct addition unless $\bm{E}_{pos}$ is implicitly extended, which is not stated. In standard ViT implementations, position embeddings are defined for all $N+1$ tokens. Correct the shape to $\mathbb{R}^{(N+1) \times D}$ and ensure alignment: 'We also add standard learnable 1D position embeddings $\bm{E}_{pos} \in \mathbb{R}^{(N+1) \times D}$ to the input embeddings.'

comments.title: Ambiguous block size sampling in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: s\leftarrow\text{Rand}(16,0.4N-\left|\mathcal{M}\right|)$ $\triangleright$ Block size
comments.explanation: The function $\text{Rand}(a,b)$ is undefined—readers cannot determine whether it samples integers or reals, and the shrinking upper bound risks invalid intervals when $|\mathcal{M}|$ approaches $0.4N$. If $0.4N - |\mathcal{M}| < 16$, no valid block size exists. This could cause algorithm failure or undefined behavior. Replace with a well-defined sampling scheme: '$s \leftarrow \text{Uniform}\{16, \min(32, 0.4N - |\mathcal{M}|)\}$' and clarify that sampling stops once $|\mathcal{M}| \geq 0.4N$.

comments.title: Incorrect softmax input dimensionality in classification
comments.quote: The category probabilities are computed as $\operatorname{softmax}(\operatorname{avg}(\{\bm{h}^{L}_{i}\}_{i=1}^{N}\bm{W}_{c}))$, where $\bm{h}^{L}_{i}$ is the final encoding vector of the $i$-th image patch, $\bm{W}_{c}\in\mathbb{R}^{D\times C}$ is a parameter matrix, and $C$ is the number of labels.
comments.explanation: The current formulation applies $\bm{W}_c$ to each patch embedding before averaging, resulting in $N$ separate $\mathbb{R}^C$ vectors that are then averaged. This is inefficient and non-standard. The typical approach is to average the $D$-dimensional patch embeddings first, then apply the classifier once. The current method introduces unnecessary parameter redundancy and may distort gradients. Rewrite as: '$\operatorname{softmax}\left( \left( \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \bm{h}^{L}_{i} \right) \bm{W}_{c} \right)$' to match standard practice and improve clarity.

comments.title: Decoder structure underspecified for segmentation
comments.quote: we use pretrained BEiT as a backbone encoder, and incorporate several deconvolution layers as decoder to produce segmentation.
comments.explanation: The term 'several deconvolution layers' lacks reproducibility—readers cannot replicate the model without knowing the number of layers, kernel sizes, strides, or normalization. SETR-PUP uses a specific upsampling pyramid; omitting these details undermines credibility. Specify the architecture: 'a 3-stage deconvolution decoder with channels [256, 128, 64], kernel size 4, and stride 2 at each stage, following SETR-PUP (Zheng et al., 2020)' to ensure fidelity.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about training from scratch in ablation
comments.quote: Compared with the results in Table 1, the ablation result is worse than training vision Transformer from scratch on two tasks.
comments.explanation: This claim contradicts the paper's main results: if BEiT outperforms from-scratch training (as stated), then an ablated version being worse than scratch training would imply the full model is flawed. More likely, the authors meant the ablated model underperforms the full BEiT model. The current phrasing misrepresents the ablation study and could mislead readers. Rewrite to: 'The ablation model performs significantly worse than the full BEiT model, indicating that visual token prediction is critical.'

comments.title: Mischaracterization of iGPT's tokenization
comments.quote: iGPT (Chen et al., 2020a) first creates a 9-bit color palette by k-means clustering RGB pixels, and then uses the clustered tokens to represent images.
comments.explanation: This misrepresents iGPT's method: it does not cluster raw RGB pixels, but applies k-means to PCA-reduced patch embeddings, forming discrete tokens via vector quantization. Describing it as simple color clustering understates its sophistication and weakens BEiT's novelty claim. Correct to: 'iGPT applies k-means clustering to PCA-projected image patches to generate discrete tokens.'

comments.title: Inaccurate claim about ViT's pixel-level auto-encoding
comments.quote:
tskiy et al. (2020) also report that pixel-level auto-encoding performs

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

worse, although it is the most straightforward translation of BERT from NLP to CV.
comments.explanation: ViT (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020) does not evaluate pixel-level auto-encoding—the paper focuses on supervised training and does not include any masked modeling experiments. This misattribution falsely positions BEiT as improving upon a known baseline that was never tested. Remove or correct the claim to reflect that no such result was reported in the cited work.

comments.title: Overstatement of methodological novelty
comments.quote: We show that the proposed method is critical to make BERT-like pre-training (i.e., auto-encoding with masked input) work well for image Transformers.
comments.explanation: The claim that BEiT is 'critical' overstates its contribution. Concurrent work like MAE (He et al., 2022) achieves strong results using pixel reconstruction without discrete tokens, showing that masked modeling can work well without BEiT's specific design. By omitting such comparisons, the paper fails to justify the necessity of its approach. Rewrite as: 'We demonstrate that our proposed method enables effective BERT-like pre-training for image Transformers' to better reflect the evidence.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to 'two views' without clarification
comments.quote: Specifically, each image has two views in our pre-training, i.e., image patches (such as $16\times 16$ pixels), and visual tokens (i.e., discrete tokens).
comments.explanation: The term 'views' is misleading—it suggests multi-view learning as in contrastive frameworks, but here it refers to input and output representations. This risks confusion with methods like SimCLR or MoCo. Replace 'two views' with 'two representations' or 'dual representations' to avoid conflation: 'Specifically, our pre-training uses two representations of each image: image patches and visual tokens.'

comments.title: Incorrect implication of novelty in masked auto-encoding
comments.quote: Motivated by BERT, we turn to the denoising auto-encoding idea to pretrain vision Transformers, which has not been well studied by the vision community.
comments.explanation: This claim is factually incorrect—iGPT (Chen et al., 2020) already explored masked auto-encoding for vision Transformers using discrete tokens. While BEiT introduces improvements, the general idea was not unexplored. Rewrite to: 'While denoising auto-encoding has been explored in vision (e.g., iGPT), we propose a new formulation using discrete visual tokens and masked image modeling tailored for vision Transformers.'

comments.title: MIM objective misstated as patch recovery
comments.quote: MIM aims at recovering the masked image patches based on encoding vectors. For
comments.explanation: The stated objective—recovering 'masked image patches'—implies pixel-level reconstruction, but BEiT predicts discrete visual tokens, not raw pixels. This misrepresents the core task. Correct to: 'MIM aims at predicting the discrete visual tokens corresponding to the masked image patches based on contextualized encoder representations.'

comments.title: Missing image and patch size specification
comments.quote: We tokenize each image to a $14\times 14$ grid of visual tokens. Notice the number of visual tokens and the number of image patches for one image are the same.
comments.explanation: The equality between token and patch count depends on $224\times224$ images and $16\times16$ patches, but this is not stated. Without specifying resolution and patch size, the alignment appears coincidental. Add: 'We tokenize each $224\times224$ image into a $14\times14$ grid of visual tokens using $16\times16$ patches, so that the number of visual tokens matches the number of image patches.'

comments.title: Incorrect sign in two-stage objective derivation
comments.quote: $\sum_{(x_{i},\tilde{x}_{i})\in\mathcal{D}}\big{(}\underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{z_{i}\sim q_{\phi}(z|x_{i})}[\log p_{\psi}(x_{i}|z_{i})]}_{\text{Stage 1: Visual Token Reconstruction}}\quad+\underbrace{\log p_{\theta}(\hat{z}_{i}|\tilde{x}_{i})}_{\text{Stage 2: Masked Image Modeling}}\big{)}$ (3)
comments.explanation: Equation (3) presents both terms as part of the stage-two objective, but the reconstruction term is fixed from stage one. This misleads readers into thinking both are optimized jointly. In reality, only $\log p_\theta$ is trained in stage two. Clarify: 'During stage two, the reconstruction term is constant; thus, maximizing the ELBO is equivalent to maximizing $\log p_\theta(\hat{z}_i|\tilde{x}_i)$, which becomes the effective training objective.'

comments.title: Ambiguous role of 'two views' in pre-training
comments.quote: The images have two views of representations in our method, namely, image patch, and visual tokens. The two types serve as input and output representations during pre-training, respectively.
comments.explanation: This phrasing oversimplifies the input: masked patches are replaced with a mask embedding, not raw patches. The input is a mixture of unmasked patch embeddings and mask tokens. Clarify: 'Image patches are converted into patch embeddings, with masked positions replaced by a learnable mask embedding; the model predicts the corresponding visual token IDs for these positions.'

comments.title: Incorrect verb tense in model input description
comments.quote: Then we randomly mask some image patches and fed them into the backbone Transformer.
comments.explanation: The verb 'fed' is past tense, while the rest of the abstract uses present tense ('we propose', 'we first tokenize'). This inconsistency disrupts the narrative. Change to 'feed' for grammatical coherence: 'Then we randomly mask some image patches and feed them into the backbone Transformer.'

comments.title: Grammatical error in input vector description
comments.quote: The input vectors $\bm{H}_{0}=[\bm{e}_{\texttt{[S]}},\bm{E}\bm{x}^{p}_{1},\ldots,\bm{E}\bm{x}^{p}_{N}]+\bm{E}_{pos}$ is fed into Transformer.
comments.explanation: The subject 'The input vectors' is plural, but the verb 'is' is singular. This subject-verb disagreement distracts from the technical content. Change 'is' to 'are': 'The input vectors ... are fed into the Transformer.'

comments.title: Misuse of 'Forth' instead of 'Fourth'
comments.quote: Forth, recovering all the visual tokens harms performance on downstream tasks.
comments.explanation: The word 'Forth' is incorrect in this context—it is an adverb of place, not an ordinal. The intended word is 'Fourth'. This typo disrupts the numbered list and undermines professionalism. Replace 'Forth' with 'Fourth'.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
BEiT introduces a masked image modeling (MIM) approach for self-supervised pre-training of vision Transformers, adapting BERT's masked language modeling to vision. The method uses a two-view approach with image patches as input and discrete visual tokens (from a dVAE tokenizer) as prediction targets. While the paper shows competitive results on ImageNet classification and ADE20K segmentation, several methodological concerns emerge regarding the tokenizer dependency, masking strategy analysis, and computational efficiency claims.

The paper makes a solid contribution by successfully adapting BERT-style pre-training to vision Transformers through discrete visual tokens. The experimental results are strong, showing improvements over contrastive methods and better scaling properties. However, the method's dependence on a separately trained tokenizer raises questions about end-to-end learning, and some claims about computational efficiency need better substantiation. The ablation studies provide useful insights but could be more comprehensive regarding the tokenizer's role.

Overview issues:

- Tokenizer dependency and generalization concerns: The paper relies on a pre-trained dVAE tokenizer from Ramesh et al. (2021) without thorough analysis of how tokenizer quality affects BEiT's performance. This creates a critical dependency that limits the method's practical applicability - users must first obtain or train a tokenizer. The paper only briefly mentions comparing with a re-implemented tokenizer in Appendix C, but the main text lacks analysis of tokenizer failure modes, vocabulary size sensitivity, or domain transfer issues. For example, it's unclear how performance degrades when using a tokenizer trained on different data distributions. This matters because the tokenizer's learned vocabulary fundamentally determines the prediction space and thus the representation learning signal.

- Insufficient masking strategy analysis: While the paper introduces blockwise masking and shows it outperforms random masking in Table 4, the analysis is cursory. The 40% masking ratio and blockwise strategy are presented without systematic exploration of alternatives. The literature context mentions that MIM focuses on high-frequency textures while contrastive learning captures global patterns, yet the paper doesn't analyze how masking ratios affect this balance. Algorithm 1's parameters (minimum block size of 16, aspect ratio range 0.3-3.3) are presented without justification or sensitivity analysis. This is important because the masking strategy directly determines the learning difficulty and the types of representations learned.

- Unsubstantiated computational efficiency claims: Section 4 claims BEiT achieves "several times of improvement in terms of pre-training throughput and memory consumption" compared to contrastive methods, but provides no supporting evidence in the main text. The claim references Appendix E, which isn't included in the provided content. This is problematic because computational efficiency is a key advantage claimed over contrastive methods that typically require large batches or memory banks. Without concrete FLOPs comparisons, training time measurements, or memory usage analysis, readers cannot evaluate this claim. This matters for practitioners considering MIM vs. contrastive approaches where compute budget is a constraint.

- Limited evaluation of tokenization quality impact: The paper doesn't analyze how the quality of learned visual tokens affects downstream performance. The dVAE tokenizer's reconstruction quality (mentioned in Section 2.1.2) could vary across image types, yet there's no analysis of whether tokenization errors propagate to BEiT's representations. The ablation in Table 4 shows that predicting visual tokens outperforms pixel prediction, but doesn't explore token granularity or vocabulary size effects. This is a significant gap because the tokenization step creates an information bottleneck - if tokens poorly represent the image content, the pre-training objective may learn suboptimal representations.

- Missing analysis of low-data regime performance: The literature context mentions that self-supervised ViTs underperform on data-insufficient downstream tasks, yet BEiT's evaluation focuses on ImageNet-scale datasets. There's no exploration of how BEiT performs when fine-tuning on smaller datasets or different domains. This is important for understanding the method's practical applicability beyond large-scale scenarios. The paper shows BEiT helps with scaling to larger models, but doesn't address whether it alleviates the data-hungry nature of ViTs in low-data regimes, which is one of the key motivations stated in the introduction.

- Missing demonstration of tokenizer failure modes: The paper relies entirely on a pre-trained dVAE tokenizer without showing what happens when tokenization fails or degrades. A complete evaluation should include experiments with deliberately corrupted or poorly trained tokenizers to demonstrate robustness. For instance, the authors could train tokenizers on limited data domains (e.g., medical images only) and show how BEiT performance degrades when transferring to natural images. Alternatively, they could simulate tokenizer errors by adding noise to the discrete tokens during pre-training. This matters because the tokenizer creates a fundamental bottleneck—if tokens cannot faithfully represent the input, the entire pre-training objective becomes compromised. The current paper assumes perfect tokenization without evidence.

- No worked example of tokenization process: The paper describes the tokenization conceptually but never shows concrete examples of how images map to discrete tokens. Readers need to see actual token sequences for sample images to understand what the model is predicting. For example, the authors could show a 14x14 grid of token indices for a few representative images, alongside the corresponding reconstructed images from the dVAE decoder. This would help readers grasp the information loss at the tokenization step and why predicting tokens might be easier than pixels. Without such examples, the abstract notion of 'visual tokens' remains opaque, making it difficult to evaluate whether the tokenization preserves semantically meaningful information.

- Missing comparison to pixel prediction baseline: While Table 4 shows that predicting visual tokens outperforms pixel prediction, the paper never presents a direct comparison with the most obvious baseline: predicting raw pixels of masked patches. The authors claim pixel prediction 'wastes modeling capability on short-range dependencies,' but provide no empirical evidence. A complete paper should include results for a pixel-level autoencoding baseline using the same masking strategy and training regime. This would directly demonstrate the claimed advantage of discrete token prediction. The absence of this baseline is particularly glaring given that predicting masked pixels is the straightforward adaptation of BERT to vision.

- No implementation details for tokenizer training: The paper uses a publicly available tokenizer but provides no guidance for researchers who need to train their own tokenizer on different domains. Critical details are missing: the dVAE architecture, training hyperparameters, convergence criteria, and computational requirements. The authors should include a complete recipe for reproducing the tokenizer training, similar to how they detail the BEiT pre-training setup. This matters because the tokenizer is not a black box—its training significantly impacts the quality of visual tokens. Without these details, researchers cannot apply BEiT to domains where the existing tokenizer is unsuitable, limiting the method's broader applicability.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add comprehensive analysis of tokenizer dependency, including sensitivity to vocabulary size, reconstruction quality, and domain transfer effects

- Provide systematic exploration of masking strategies, including ratio sensitivity and blockwise parameter ablations

- Include concrete computational efficiency comparisons with contrastive baselines, measuring FLOPs, training time, and memory usage

- Evaluate BEiT in low-data fine-tuning regimes to address the data-hungry concern raised in the introduction

- Analyze how tokenization quality affects representation learning through controlled experiments with varying tokenizer performance

comments.title: Algorithm 1 termination condition overshoots target
comments.quote: until $\left|\mathcal{M}\right|>0.4N$ $\triangleright$ Masking ratio is $40\%$
comments.explanation: The termination condition will continue adding blocks until the masked set exceeds 40% of patches, potentially resulting in significantly more than the intended 40% masked patches. Since blocks have variable size, this could substantially overshoot the target. Change the condition to $\left|\mathcal{M}\right| \geq 0.4N$ or implement logic to ensure exactly $\left|\mathcal{M}\right| = 0.4N$ by adjusting the final block size.

comments.title: Block size calculation invalid near completion
comments.quote: $s\leftarrow\text{Rand}(16,0.4N-\left|\mathcal{M}\right|)$ $\triangleright$ Block size
comments.explanation: When $\left|\mathcal{M}\right|$ approaches $0.4N$, the upper bound $0.4N - \left|\mathcal{M}\right|$ can become less than the minimum block size of 16, making the random selection invalid. This edge case needs handling. Add a check to ensure $0.4N - \left|\mathcal{M}\right| \geq 16$ before sampling, or adjust the final block to use the remaining patches.

comments.title: Block boundary validation missing
comments.quote:
$t\leftarrow\text{Rand}(0,h-a);l\leftarrow\text{Rand}(0,w-b)$
$\mathcal{M}\leftarrow\mathcal{M}\bigcup\{(i,j):i\in[t,t+a),j\in[l,l+b)\}$
comments.explanation: The algorithm doesn't ensure integer indices for block boundaries. Since $a = \sqrt{s \cdot r}$ and $b = \sqrt{s/r}$ may be non-integer, and $t, l$ are sampled continuously, the resulting indices may not be valid integer positions. Add integer rounding (e.g., $a = \lfloor\sqrt{s \cdot r}\rfloor$) and validate that all indices in $\mathcal{M}$ are within the $h \times w$ grid.

comments.title: Position embedding dimension mismatch
comments.quote: We also add standard learnable 1D position embeddings $\bm{E}_{pos}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times D}$ to patch embeddings. The input vectors $\bm{H}_{0}=[\bm{e}_{\texttt{[S]}},\bm{E}\bm{x}^{p}_{i},\ldots,\bm{E}\bm{x}^{p}_{N}]+\bm{E}_{pos}$ is fed into Transformer.
comments.explanation: The input sequence contains N+1 elements (N patches plus the [S] token), but $\bm{E}_{pos}$ only provides embeddings for N elements. This creates a shape mismatch when adding position embeddings. Define $\bm{E}_{pos}\in\mathbb{R}^{(N+1)\times D}$ to include a position embedding for the [S] token.

comments.title: Mathematical error in classification formula
comments.quote: The category probabilities are computed as $\operatorname{softmax}(\operatorname{avg}(\{\bm{h}_{i}^{L}\}_{i=1}^{N}\bm{W}_{c}))$
comments.explanation: The formula incorrectly applies the linear transformation before averaging. Based on the text description of using average pooling first, the correct formula should be $\operatorname{softmax}(\operatorname{avg}(\{\bm{h}_{i}^{L}\}_{i=1}^{N})\bm{W}_{c})$, where the average pooling produces a single D-dimensional vector before multiplication by $\bm{W}_{c}$.

comments.title: Incorrect masking set notation
comments.quote: where the masked positions are denoted as $\mathcal{M}\in\{1,\dots,N\}^{0.4N}$
comments.explanation: The notation $\mathcal{M}\in\{1,\dots,N\}^{0.4N}$ suggests $\mathcal{M}$ is a tuple of length $0.4N$, but it should be a subset of indices. Use $\mathcal{M}\subseteq\{1,\dots,N\}$ with $|\mathcal{M}|=0.4N$ instead.

comments.title: Aspect ratio range inconsistency
comments.quote: $r\leftarrow\text{Rand}(0.3,\frac{1}{0.3})$ $\triangleright$ Aspect ratio of block
comments.explanation: The algorithm uses $\frac{1}{0.3} = 3.\overline{3}$ while the text states the range is 0.3 to 3.3. This minor discrepancy could cause implementation issues. Either change the algorithm to use $\text{Rand}(0.3, 3.3)$ or update the text to reflect the exact reciprocal value.

comments.title: Adam optimizer citation needs correction
comments.quote: Adam *(Loshchilov and Hutter, 2019)* with $\beta_{1}=0.9,\beta_{2}=0.999$ is employed for optimization.
comments.explanation: Loshchilov and Hutter (2019) introduced AdamW, which decouples weight decay. Since the paper uses weight decay separately, this matches AdamW. Change the citation to 'AdamW *(Loshchilov and Hutter, 2019)*' for accuracy.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
BEiT proposes a masked image modeling (MIM) framework for vision Transformers, utilizing discrete visual tokens from a pre-trained dVAE as the prediction target rather than raw pixels. The paper demonstrates strong performance on image classification and semantic segmentation, arguing that this token-level objective better captures high-level visual semantics.

The paper makes a significant contribution by successfully adapting BERT-style masked language modeling to the vision domain. The approach is conceptually elegant and provides a strong baseline for self-supervised learning, though it relies heavily on the quality of the external dVAE tokenizer.

Overview issues:

- Dependency on External Tokenizer Quality: The entire BEiT framework relies on a pre-trained dVAE tokenizer to provide discrete visual tokens. Section 2.1.2 notes that the authors use a publicly available tokenizer from Ramesh et al. (2021), but the paper lacks a rigorous analysis of how the quality, vocabulary size, or semantic granularity of this tokenizer impacts the final downstream performance. If the tokenizer is sub-optimal, the MIM objective might be learning to predict noise or low-level artifacts rather than meaningful semantic structures. To address this, the authors should provide an ablation study comparing performance across different tokenizer configurations or discuss the sensitivity of the model to the tokenizer's latent space properties.

- Lack of Theoretical Justification for Masking Strategy: Section 2.3 introduces blockwise masking as a key component for performance, yet the choice of a 40% masking ratio and the specific block size heuristic (Algorithm 1) appears largely empirical. The paper claims this avoids short-range dependencies, but there is no formal or empirical investigation into whether this masking pattern is optimal for the spatial continuity of image data compared to other strategies like random patch masking or attention-guided masking. Adding a comparison between blockwise masking and alternative spatial masking patterns would strengthen the claim that this specific approach is necessary for capturing long-range dependencies.

- Unclear Generalization to Non-Natural Images: The evaluation is restricted to natural image datasets (ImageNet-1K, ADE20K), which are characterized by high object-centric semantic content. The authors claim the model learns to distinguish semantic regions, but it remains unclear if this holds for domains where object boundaries are less defined or where texture is the primary signal, such as medical imagery or satellite data.
